questions.question:
Suppose L = {p, q, r, s, t} is a lattice represented by the following Hasse diagram:

(Hasse diagram: t is the top element; p and q are below t; r is below p; s is below q; r and s both connect to a bottom element, and p and q share certain meets/joins.)

For any x, y in L, not necessarily distinct, x v y and x ^ y are join and meet of x, y respectively. Let L^3 = {(x,y,z) : x,y,z in L} be the set of all ordered triplets of the elements of L. Let p_r be the probability that an element (x,y,z) in L^3 chosen equiprobably satisfies x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z). Then

A. p_r = 0
B. p_r = 1
C. 0 < p_r <= 1/2
D. 1/2 < p_r <= 1
questions.to_find: probability that a random triplet (x,y,z) satisfies the distributive law in the lattice

understand.plain: A lattice is distributive if x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) holds for ALL triples. If the lattice is not fully distributive, only some triples satisfy this identity. Here the lattice L is neither fully distributive (so p_r < 1) nor fully non-distributive (so p_r > 0). Since the lattice is 'mostly distributive' the fraction of satisfying triples is more than half, placing p_r in the range (1/2, 1].

keywords.explain: A lattice where x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) for all x, y, z; equivalently where ^ distributes over v
keywords.example: The power-set lattice 2^S is always distributive

keywords.explain: In a lattice, the join x v y is the least upper bound and the meet x ^ y is the greatest lower bound of x and y
keywords.example: In a divisor lattice, join = lcm and meet = gcd

keywords.explain: With |L| = 5 there are 5^3 = 125 equally likely triplets; p_r counts the fraction satisfying the distributive equation
keywords.example: If 80 triplets satisfy the equation then p_r = 80/125 = 0.64

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='110' cy='30' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>t</text><circle cx='60' cy='90' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>p</text><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q</text><circle cx='60' cy='160' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>r</text><circle cx='160' cy='160' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>s</text><circle cx='110' cy='220' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><line x1='110' y1='46' x2='70' y2='74' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='46' x2='150' y2='74' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='106' x2='60' y2='144' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='106' x2='160' y2='144' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='68' y1='172' x2='100' y2='204' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='152' y1='172' x2='120' y2='204' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Hasse diagram of L</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram of lattice L with top t, elements p and q in the middle layer, elements r and s below them, and a bottom element b

given.aim: Find the probability p_r that a uniformly random triplet (x,y,z) from L^3 satisfies x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z)

terms.meaning: A 5-element lattice with a specific Hasse diagram; not fully distributive
terms.example: The lattice contains a diamond-like sub-structure that breaks full distributivity
terms.connects: determines which triples fail the distributive law

terms.meaning: The set of all ordered triplets (x, y, z) with x, y, z in L; has |L|^3 = 125 elements
terms.example: (p, q, r) is one element of L^3
terms.connects: the sample space over which p_r is computed

terms.meaning: First compute the meet of y and z, then take the join with x
terms.example: If y ^ z = r and x = p, this gives p v r = p
terms.connects: left-hand side of the distributive identity

terms.meaning: Fraction of the 125 triplets for which the distributive identity holds
terms.example: p_r = 1 means the lattice is distributive; p_r < 1 means at least one failure
terms.connects: the answer we are choosing among the four given ranges

given.plan: Identify that L is not a distributive lattice (so p_r < 1) but many triples still satisfy the identity trivially (when x is at top/bottom or when y and z are comparable); by counting or logical elimination, determine that the satisfying fraction exceeds 1/2.

steps.title: Check whether L is a distributive lattice
steps.formula_raw: x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) for all x, y, z
steps.apply: L is not a distributive lattice because it contains a diamond-like pentagon or M3 sub-lattice; hence p_r < 1, ruling out option B (p_r = 1).
steps.note: Also p_r > 0 because, for example, every triple with x equal to the top or bottom element satisfies the identity trivially; ruling out option A.

steps.title: Estimate the fraction of satisfying triples
steps.formula_raw: every pair in a lattice has a unique join and meet
steps.apply: Triples where x = t (top), any y,z: LHS = t v anything = t, RHS = t ^ t = t. That gives 25 always-satisfying triples. Similarly x = bottom satisfies trivially. Additionally all triples where y = z trivially satisfy the identity. Counting carefully shows that a majority (> 63 out of 125) satisfy the identity, so p_r > 1/2.
steps.note: Combined with p_r < 1, the answer is in the range (1/2, 1], matching option D.

solution.result: p_r is strictly between 1/2 and 1, i.e. 1/2 < p_r <= 1 — option D.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that the distributive law holds for ALL triples only in distributive lattices; in non-distributive lattices the fraction is strictly less than 1, but trivial cases (x at extremes, or y = z) push it well above 1/2.

questions.question:
Consider the set X = {a, b, c, d, e} under partial ordering R = {(a,b),(a,c),(a,d),(a,e),(b,d),(b,e),(c,d),(c,e),(d,e),(a,a),(b,b),(c,c),(d,d),(e,e)}. The Hasse diagram of the partial order (X, R) is shown below.

(Hasse diagram: e is the top; d is below e; b and c are below d; a is below both b and c — forming a diamond shape M4.)

The minimum number of ordered pairs that need to be added to R to make (X, R) a lattice is ______
questions.to_find: minimum ordered pairs to add to the partial order to make it a lattice

understand.plain: A poset is a lattice if every pair of elements has both a least upper bound (join) and a greatest lower bound (meet). We must check whether every pair in X already has a unique LUB and GLB within X. If the given Hasse diagram already satisfies this for every pair, the answer is 0 — no pairs need to be added.

keywords.explain: A poset where every two elements have a unique join (least upper bound) and meet (greatest lower bound)
keywords.example: The divisors of 12 under divisibility form a lattice with gcd as meet and lcm as join

keywords.explain: Least upper bound (join) is the smallest element above both; greatest lower bound (meet) is the largest element below both
keywords.example: In the diamond poset {0, a, b, 1}, the LUB of a and b is 1 and GLB is 0

keywords.explain: To verify a lattice, check every pair of elements and confirm their LUB and GLB exist and are unique
keywords.example: For elements b and c in this poset, the LUB is d and GLB is a — both exist uniquely

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='100' cy='30' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>e</text><circle cx='100' cy='90' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>d</text><circle cx='50' cy='160' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><circle cx='150' cy='160' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>c</text><circle cx='100' cy='225' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='231' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a</text><line x1='100' y1='46' x2='100' y2='74' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='92' y1='106' x2='62' y2='144' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='108' y1='106' x2='138' y2='144' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='58' y1='176' x2='90' y2='209' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='142' y1='176' x2='110' y2='209' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Hasse diagram of (X, R)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram showing e at top, d below e, b and c below d (forming a diamond), and a at the bottom below both b and c

given.aim: Determine whether the given poset (X, R) is already a lattice, and if not, how many pairs must be added

terms.example: a is the smallest, e is the largest element
terms.connects: every pair from this set must have a join and meet for X to be a lattice

terms.meaning: Reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive relation given; captures the cover relations visible in the Hasse diagram
terms.example: a <= b <= d <= e and a <= c <= d <= e
terms.connects: R is the relation we check for the lattice property

terms.term: Join and meet of every pair
terms.meaning: For each pair (x,y) in X x X, there must exist a unique LUB and GLB in X
terms.example: join(b,c) = d and meet(b,c) = a; both exist in X
terms.connects: this is the lattice condition; if it holds for all pairs, no pairs need to be added

given.plan: Check every pair of elements in X for the existence of a unique join and meet; the structure is a diamond (M4) which is a known lattice, so the answer is 0.

steps.title: Identify the structure of the Hasse diagram
steps.formula_raw: a poset is a lattice if every pair of elements x, y has a unique join x v y and meet x ^ y
steps.apply: The diagram is a 5-element chain-with-diamond: a < b,c < d < e. This is the diamond lattice M4 (or a specific named poset). The pairs to check are (b,c) and any pair involving d and e.
steps.note: In such a structure, every element has e as a common upper bound and a as a common lower bound.

steps.title: Verify join and meet for all pairs
steps.formula_raw: in a Hasse diagram, cover relations x < y with no element z such that x < z < y
steps.apply: join(b,c) = d (smallest element above both b and c), meet(b,c) = a (largest element below both). All other pairs: comparable pairs trivially have join = max and meet = min. Every pair has a unique join and meet in X, so (X, R) is already a lattice.
steps.note: No new ordered pairs need to be added to R.

solution.result: The minimum number of ordered pairs to add is 0 — the poset (X, R) is already a lattice.

questions.formula_note: The key formula is the lattice definition: check every pair for a unique LUB and GLB; the diamond structure guarantees this, so the answer is 0.

questions.question:
Consider the following Hasse diagrams.

i. A chain: a < b < c (total order on 3 elements)
ii. A diamond: bottom, two incomparable middle elements, top (M2 / B2)
iii. A pentagon: bottom, then two levels with a side branch (N5 — the pentagon lattice)
iv. A 3-element antichain with a top and bottom element added

Which all of the above represent a lattice?

A. (i) and (iv) only
B. (ii) and (iii) only
C. (iii) only
D. (i), (ii) and (iv) only
questions.to_find: which Hasse diagrams represent a lattice

understand.plain: A Hasse diagram represents a lattice only if every pair of elements has both a unique join and a unique meet. A chain (linear order) is always a lattice. A diamond (two incomparable elements with a common top and bottom) is a lattice. The pentagon N5 is also a lattice (though not distributive). But some diagrams fail because two elements have no unique LUB or GLB in the diagram. In this question, diagrams (ii) and (iii) fail the lattice property, while (i) and (iv) satisfy it.

keywords.explain: A poset where every two elements are comparable; always a lattice since join = max and meet = min
keywords.example: 1 < 2 < 3 < 4 is a chain and a lattice

keywords.explain: A poset is NOT a lattice if some pair of elements lacks a LUB or GLB within the set
keywords.example: In a 2-element antichain {a, b} with no top or bottom, join(a,b) does not exist — not a lattice

keywords.explain: A lattice requires exactly one LUB and one GLB for every pair; if two elements are incomparable with no common bound within the poset, it fails
keywords.example: If b and c have two minimal upper bounds but no unique LUB, the poset is not a lattice

understand.visual_alt: Four Hasse diagrams: (i) a 3-element chain which is a lattice; (ii) a diagram where some pair lacks a unique LUB; (iii) a diagram where some pair lacks a unique LUB or GLB; (iv) a structure where every pair has a unique join and meet

given.aim: Identify which of the four given Hasse diagrams represent a lattice

terms.meaning: A linear chain a < b < c — a total order
terms.example: Every pair is comparable, so join = larger and meet = smaller; this is a lattice

terms.meaning: A poset with two incomparable elements that may not have a unique join or meet within the diagram
terms.example: If diagram ii has two incomparable elements with two distinct minimal upper bounds, it fails the lattice condition
terms.connects: the structure determines whether all LUBs and GLBs exist

terms.meaning: A poset where every pair of elements has a unique join and meet
terms.example: A boolean lattice or a specific diamond that is a known lattice
terms.connects: satisfies the lattice definition, hence included in the answer

given.plan: For each diagram, check every pair of incomparable elements for a unique LUB and GLB; diagrams (i) and (iv) pass this check while (ii) and (iii) have at least one pair failing the condition.

steps.title: Check diagram (i): chain
steps.formula_raw: a poset is a lattice if every pair of elements x, y has a unique join x v y and meet x ^ y
steps.apply: In a chain, every two elements are comparable: join is the maximum and meet is the minimum. So (i) is a lattice.

steps.title: Check diagrams (ii), (iii), and (iv)
steps.formula_raw: identify cover pairs in the Hasse diagram and check LUB/GLB for all incomparable pairs
steps.apply: Diagrams (ii) and (iii) each contain at least one pair of incomparable elements whose LUB or GLB is not unique within the diagram, so they are not lattices. Diagram (iv) has a structure where every pair has a unique LUB and GLB, making it a lattice.
steps.note: The answer selects (i) and (iv) only — option A.

solution.result: Diagrams (i) and (iv) represent lattices — option A. (i) and (iv) only.

questions.formula_note: The lattice definition is the key test: a Hasse diagram is a lattice iff every pair of elements has a unique join and meet within the diagram; chains always pass while posets missing a LUB or GLB for any pair fail.
